framework: A path qualifies only when >=3 prominent, proven people in the field visibly share the capability, each with a resolving link — the pattern across standouts is the evidence, not market demand. Ranked by strength of that pattern. Curated editorial, not DOL-derived.

paths.path: Build and open-source a security tool people actually run
paths.what_it_is: Write and publicly maintain a scanner, framework, or detection-rule set (an exploitation framework, a YARA/Sigma rule set, an IOC scanner, a Linux security distro) that other practitioners install and depend on.
paths.why_it_signals: The standouts here — HD Moore, Florian Roth, and Mati Aharoni — each turned a personal tool into industry-standard infrastructure, which proves an employer-grade combination of technical depth, follow-through, and community trust that a resume line can't fake.

paths.path: Hunt vulnerabilities and publish public writeups of the findings
paths.what_it_is: Find real bugs through bug-bounty programs and publish detailed, technical writeups of how each one was discovered and exploited, building a public track record over time.
paths.why_it_signals: STÖK, Ben Sadeghipour (NahamSec), and Frans Rosén all built their careers the same way — hunting live targets and narrating the methodology in public — which shows an employer the exact offense-minded, self-directed research skill they'd otherwise have to take on faith.

paths.path: Compete in CTFs and teach what you learned in public
paths.what_it_is: Play Capture-the-Flag competitions regularly and publish walkthroughs (video or written) that explain the exploitation technique step by step, not just the flag.
paths.why_it_signals: LiveOverflow (Fabian Fäßler), John Hammond, and Perri Adams all paired competitive CTF play with public teaching, showing employers a rare double signal: hands-on offensive skill plus the ability to explain it clearly, which is exactly what strong SOC and red-team hires need.
